behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: Real failures occur when the LLM misses terms due to unusual phrasing, structural complexity, or semantic ambiguity—not just obvious direct matches. A mock that returns a fixed set of extracted terms would always pass but wouldn't catch the core failure: can the LLM reliably extract clauses from diverse contract structures? Testing with real LLM calls against realistic contracts is the only way to verify the system 'identifies all' terms it should find.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the actual clause extractor against realistic legal contracts with varied clause formats and phrasings (standard templates, complex multi-party agreements, non-standard terms). Verify the extractor identifies all expected clause types (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) that a competent human would flag. Crucially, confirm the system preserves every extracted term in the final report without truncation or loss, even when the LLM returns unusual formats or large volumes of terms.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real LLM calls to verify behavior against diverse contract structures that mocks would not expose.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is LLM comprehension of indirect/euphemistic risk signals. A mock that returns hardcoded flags hides the real challenge: semantic understanding. For example, 'the party may choose to discontinue engagement at their discretion' is an indirect way to express an undesired opt-out clause under many policies. Only a real LLM reasoning about contract semantics and policy intent can reliably detect this. Mocks would never reveal failures to detect indirect risks.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the risk assessor on contracts containing both explicit risk language and euphemistic/indirect problem phrasing. Test cases: vague obligation language ('reasonable efforts'), disguised opt-out clauses, penalty clauses phrased as standard terms, etc. For each flagged risk, verify the system records severity, reasoning, and policy basis. Test against different configured risk policies to confirm policy-aware detection. Critically, verify the system preserves all flagged findings with supporting context in the final report.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Detecting euphemistic language requires real LLM semantic understanding; no mock can reliably simulate this without predefined examples that wouldn't generalize.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: Correctness hinges on the LLM understanding both the contract and jurisdiction-specific regulatory requirements. A mock could always return 'compliant=true' and never expose real failures: missed compliance gaps, misinterpreted requirements, or subtle regulatory mismatches. Real failures only appear when the LLM reasons about a real contract against real regulatory requirements. Testing with mocked LLMs would not catch fundamental comprehension failures.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the compliance checker on contracts for multiple jurisdictions (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, California consumer law, general commercial). For each jurisdiction, include test contracts that meet requirements and contracts with known compliance gaps (missing data retention policies, inadequate liability clauses, etc.). Verify the checker identifies whether all requirements are met and provides reasoning for any gaps (e.g., 'missing GDPR data retention policy'). Confirm the final report contains the compliance determination with supporting evidence for all jurisdictions tested.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Understanding jurisdiction-specific compliance requires real LLM reasoning; mocks cannot reliably simulate nuanced regulatory interpretation.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: This is pure merge logic, not LLM capability. The failure mode is algorithmic: merge logic incorrectly selects one assessment and discards the other, loses attribution information, or corrupts the output format. These failures can be caught at Integration level by testing the merge function with realistic mocked inputs that deliberately conflict. No LLM capability testing is needed; the merge function combines structured outputs from the reviewers.
behaviors.test_description: Create mocked outputs from all three reviewers where they explicitly disagree on the same clause. Example: clause extraction says 'standard payment term (30 days),' risk assessor flags the same clause as 'high risk' due to late payment penalties, compliance checker says 'acceptable under jurisdiction X.' Run merge and verify the final report contains all three assessments with unambiguous attribution (e.g., '[Risk Assessor] high risk because: late payment penalties exceed...'). Test multiple conflict scenarios: all three disagreeing, two agreeing against one, etc. Verify no assessment is lost and no attribution is scrambled or ambiguous.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Merge logic is testable at Integration with realistic mocked inputs; no LLM dependency required.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: This is a full agentic pipeline with multiple steps (dispatch clause extractor, dispatch risk assessor, dispatch compliance checker, merge results). The failure boundary is 'findings are lost during orchestration' or 'concurrent execution doesn't collect all results.' While orchestration logic could be tested at System level with mocked LLMs, realistic testing of a full pipeline requires Workflow level: verifying all three agentic reviewers execute correctly, all findings from each are collected before merge, timeouts are handled, and no data is lost across the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full pipeline end-to-end on realistic legal contracts of varying complexity. Verify all three reviewers are dispatched concurrently and complete within configured timeout. Confirm that all results from each reviewer are collected (no partial results or dropped findings). Verify the merge step receives complete input from all three reviewers and produces a unified report containing all clause extractions, all risk flags, and all compliance determinations. Test with contracts of different sizes and complexity to ensure pipeline integrity scales. Include error scenarios: one reviewer times out, one reviewer returns a malformed response, partial failures. Verify the system either gracefully degradates (partial report with available findings) or retries/handles errors without losing already-collected findings.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing a full agentic pipeline requires Workflow-level thinking to verify orchestration, concurrency, and multi-step coordination work end-to-end with realistic findings.
